agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Real-time courier tracking feature for customer-facing delivery experience. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements regarding courier location visibility — one demands full transparency, the other demands zero visibility. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that cannot be resolved at the engineering level.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Customer Experience","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver contracted or employed to transport packages from origin to customer destination."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"GPS coordinates of a courier's device, updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The window of time between a courier picking up a package and completing the delivery to the customer."},{"term":"Location visibility","definition":"The degree to which a courier's position is exposed to the end customer — ranges from exact GPS coordinates to no visibility at all."},{"term":"Courier retention","definition":"The ability to keep delivery drivers working on the platform; a key operational metric directly affected by driver satisfaction and perceived working conditions."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for an extended period (e.g., traffic, break) — exact GPS makes it look like they are not working, triggering customer complaints","expected_behavior":"Cannot define without resolving the core conflict"},{"scenario":"Courier is near customer's home but delivering another order first (multi-stop route) — customer sees courier 'right there' but not arriving","expected_behavior":"Cannot define without resolving the core conflict"},{"scenario":"Courier location data persists after delivery — potential privacy/safety issue if customer can see where courier went next","expected_behavior":"Courier location must never be accessible after delivery completion regardless of which policy wins"},{"scenario":"Courier manually disables GPS on their device","expected_behavior":"Cannot define without resolving the core conflict — is this acceptable driver autonomy or a policy violation?"},{"scenario":"Courier is in a sensitive location (hospital, domestic violence shelter) during multi-stop delivery","expected_behavior":"Even under full transparency policy, there may be legal/safety obligations to suppress location in certain contexts"}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk either way. Full transparency may reduce customer support contacts ('where is my package?') saving cost, but losing couriers increases recruitment and training costs which typically dwarf support savings. Courier churn in the logistics industry costs $3,000-$7,000 per replacement depending on market.","operational":"If couriers leave due to surveillance concerns, delivery capacity drops, SLAs are missed, and remaining couriers are overloaded — creating a death spiral. If customers have zero visibility, inbound 'where is my order?' volume increases, straining support teams.","user":"Customers want delivery predictability. Couriers want dignity and privacy during work. Both are legitimate user groups. Optimizing for one at the total expense of the other is not sustainable.","risk":"CRITICAL: This is also a labor law and privacy regulation risk. In multiple jurisdictions (EU GDPR, California CCPA, various labor laws), sharing a worker's exact real-time GPS coordinates with third parties may require explicit consent and have legal constraints. No legal review has been cited by either team.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — delivery experience and perceived transparency depend on resolution"},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected — workplace privacy, sense of autonomy, and retention are at stake"},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Responsible for courier supply and delivery capacity; courier churn threatens their core metrics"},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Responsible for customer satisfaction and NPS; lack of visibility may increase complaints"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED — this is a major gap. Real-time worker location sharing has regulatory implications"},{"group":"Product leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict — this is a product strategy decision, not an engineering or team-level decision"}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Two authoritative internal teams have submitted directly contradictory non-negotiable requirements. This is not an ambiguity or a gap — it is a hard contradiction. No acceptance criteria can be defined because any criteria would satisfy one team while violating the other's stated constraint. Additionally, no legal review has been performed on the privacy and labor law implications, which could override both teams' positions. Confidence is well below 50.","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine business policy contradiction that cannot be resolved by engineering","Courier retention is a real and expensive problem in the logistics industry","There are legal/privacy dimensions neither team has addressed","A compromise solution likely exists (e.g., zone-based tracking, ETA-only, proximity indicators) but neither team has been asked about acceptable middle ground"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder group's needs should take priority — this is a business strategy decision","Legal and regulatory constraints on courier location sharing in the relevant jurisdictions","Whether couriers would accept any form of location sharing, even approximate","Whether customers actually need exact GPS or would be satisfied with ETA and status updates"],"assumptions":["Both teams are accurately representing their stakeholders' positions","No legal review has been conducted on real-time worker location sharing","There is no existing company policy or executive directive that already resolves this conflict"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS visibility at all times (non-negotiable). Operations requires zero location visibility (non-negotiable). These requirements are mutually exclusive. Engineering cannot build a feature that simultaneously shows and hides courier location. This must be resolved by product leadership or executive decision-maker before any implementation begins.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Operations team","Customer Success team","Product leadership"],"options":["A: Full GPS transparency (Customer Success wins, Operations loses — courier retention risk)","B: Zero visibility (Operations wins, Customer Success loses — customer experience gap)","C: Compromise — show approximate location (zone/neighborhood level), ETA, or delivery status without exact coordinates. Requires BOTH teams to revisit their 'non-negotiable' stance.","D: Courier-controlled visibility — let couriers opt in/out of location sharing, with incentives for opting in. Respects driver autonomy while offering transparency where available."],"recommendation":"Option C or D. The logistics industry has largely converged on approximate location (moving dot on a map at zone level) plus live ETA as the standard. This gives customers delivery predictability without surveilling drivers at GPS precision. However, this is a PRODUCT STRATEGY decision — I recommend escalating to the VP of Product or equivalent with both teams present. Neither team should be overruled without hearing the other's constraints. Additionally, Legal MUST review before any location-sharing feature ships."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal or compliance review has been cited by either team. Sharing a worker's exact real-time GPS coordinates with customers has significant legal implications under GDPR (legitimate interest vs. worker privacy), CCPA, and various labor protection laws. This could constrain or entirely dictate the solution regardless of either team's preference.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers","Product leadership"],"options":["Engage Legal/Compliance for a privacy impact assessment before defining requirements","Proceed without legal review (NOT recommended)"],"recommendation":"Mandatory legal review before any requirements are finalized. In the EU, a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) would likely be required for real-time worker tracking exposed to third parties."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement — contradictory requirements from two authoritative stakeholders","business_rule_broken":"No business rules can be defined when the fundamental policy question (show vs. hide courier location) is unresolved"},{"violation":"Missing legal/compliance review for worker location sharing","business_rule_broken":"Any implementation of real-time location sharing without legal review risks regulatory violation and potential liability"}]}
